words.m: verb aorist active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Simeon Peter, a bondservant and apostle of Jesus Christ, to those who have obtained a faith of equal honor with us in the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ:
verses.note: GREEK: "Symeon" (Συμεών) preserves the Hebrew form of Simon, a Semitic spelling found also in Acts 15:14, lending authenticity to Petrine authorship. "Isotimon" (ἰσότιμον, Strong's G2472) is a hapax legomenon in the NT meaning "of equal honor/value," emphasizing that Gentile believers possess a faith no less precious than that of the apostles. THEOLOGICAL: "Our God and Savior Jesus Christ" (τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν καὶ σωτῆρος Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ) follows the Granville Sharp rule: a single article governing two nouns joined by "kai" with the same case refers to one person—thus directly identifying Jesus Christ as God and Savior (cf. Titus 2:13).

verses.flow: Grace to you and peace be multiplied in the full knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord,
verses.note: GREEK: "Epignōsis" (ἐπίγνωσις, Strong's G1922) is intensified "gnōsis"—not mere knowledge but full, experiential, relational knowledge. Peter uses this term four times in this chapter (vv. 2, 3, 8, cf. v. 20), making it a programmatic keyword. Grace and peace are multiplied not through ritual but through deepening knowledge of God and Jesus the Lord.

words.m: verb perfect middle participle genitive singular feminine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: seeing that His divine power has granted to us all things pertaining to life and godliness, through the full knowledge of the One having called us by His own glory and virtue,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "His divine power" (τῆς θείας δυνάμεως αὐτοῦ) indicates that everything necessary for spiritual life and godliness has already been granted—a completed gift (perfect participle δεδωρημένης). GREEK: "Theios" (θεῖος, Strong's G2304) meaning "divine" appears in the NT only here, v. 4, and Acts 17:29, showing Peter engaging Hellenistic vocabulary to communicate transcendent realities. "Aretē" (ἀρετή) here means "moral excellence" or "manifested power," echoing LXX usage for God's mighty deeds (Isa 42:12).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: through which He has granted to us the precious and exceedingly great promises, so that through these you might become partakers of the divine nature, having escaped the corruption that is in the world through desire,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Partakers of the divine nature" (θείας κοινωνοὶ φύσεως) is one of the most remarkable statements in the NT—believers participate in God's own nature through His promises. This is not pantheistic absorption but covenantal transformation. GREEK: "Apophygontes" (ἀποφυγόντες, Strong's G668) means "having escaped," an aorist participle indicating a decisive break from the world's corruption. "Phthora" (φθορά) denotes decay and moral ruin, the opposite of divine incorruptibility (cf. 2 Pet 2:12, 19).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And for this very reason also, having brought in all diligence, supply in your faith virtue, and in virtue knowledge,
and in knowledge self-control, and in self-control endurance, and in endurance godliness,
and in godliness brotherly affection, and in brotherly affection love.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: For these things being yours and abounding, they render you neither idle nor unfruitful unto the full knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For he in whom these things are not present is blind, being short-sighted, having received forgetfulness of the cleansing of his former sins.
verses.note: GREEK: "Myōpazōn" (μυωπάζων, Strong's G3467) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "being short-sighted" or "squinting"—one who can see only what is near but not the far-reaching realities of salvation. Combined with "blind" (τυφλός), Peter describes a progressive spiritual dysfunction. THEOLOGICAL: Forgetting one's cleansing from former sins produces moral blindness; sanctification requires active remembrance of justification (cf. Rom 6:1-11).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, brothers, be all the more diligent to make your calling and election firm; for doing these things, you shall never stumble.
For in this way there shall be richly supplied to you the entrance into the eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ" (τὴν αἰώνιον βασιλείαν τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν καὶ σωτῆρος Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ) again applies the Granville Sharp construction, identifying Jesus as both Lord and Savior who reigns over an everlasting dominion. TYPOLOGY: "Entrance" (εἴσοδος) echoes Israel's entry into the Promised Land; as Joshua led Israel in, so Jesus the true Joshua leads believers into the eternal kingdom (cf. Heb 4:8-11). The adverb "richly" (πλουσίως) suggests not bare entry but an abundant, triumphant welcome.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: Therefore I shall be ready always to remind you concerning these things, though you know them and have been established in the present truth.
But I consider it right, for as long as I am in this tabernacle, to stir you up by way of reminder,

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: knowing that the putting off of my tabernacle is swift, even as also our Lord Jesus Christ made clear to me.
And I will be diligent also that at each time you may be able, after my departure, to make remembrance of these things.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For not having followed cunningly devised myths did we make known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but having become eyewitnesses of that One's majesty.
verses.note: GREEK: "Epoptai" (ἐπόπται, Strong's G2030) means "eyewitnesses" but carries the connotation of initiated observers in Greek mystery religions—Peter repurposes this term to assert that the apostles witnessed no fabricated myth but real divine disclosure. "Sesophismenois mythois" (σεσοφισμένοις μύθοις) means "cleverly devised myths," contrasting pagan fabrications with apostolic testimony. HISTORICAL: Peter references the Transfiguration (Matt 17:1-8; Mark 9:2-8; Luke 9:28-36) as empirical verification of Christ's divine majesty and future parousia.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: For having received from God the Father honor and glory, when such a voice was borne to Him by the Majestic Glory: "This is My Son, My Beloved, in whom I am well pleased"—
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The divine voice echoes the baptism of Jesus (Matt 3:17) and recalls Psalm 2:7 ("You are My Son") and Isaiah 42:1 ("My Chosen One in whom My soul delights"), uniting royal Messianic sonship with the Suffering Servant identity. GREEK: "Megaloprepous doxēs" (μεγαλοπρεποῦς δόξης, "Majestic Glory") is a circumlocution for God's manifest presence—"megaloprepēs" (Strong's G3169) is a NT hapax, meaning "befitting greatness." This recalls the kavod (כָּבוֹד) of YHWH descending in theophanic cloud, as at Sinai and the Tabernacle (Exod 24:16-17).

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and this voice we ourselves heard borne out of heaven, being with Him on the holy mountain.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And we have the prophetic word made more sure, to which you do well to give heed, as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prophetic word is "made more sure" (βεβαιότερον)—the Transfiguration confirmed what the prophets foretold, not replacing Scripture but corroborating it. GREEK: "Phōsphoros" (φωσφόρος, Strong's G5459), literally "light-bearer," refers to the morning star (Venus), a Messianic title (cf. Rev 22:16; Num 24:17). "Auchmēros" (αὐχμηρός, Strong's G850) is a NT hapax meaning "dry, murky, squalid"—depicting the present age as a dark, parched place awaiting the dawn of Christ's return. TYPOLOGY: The lamp in darkness echoes Psalm 119:105 and anticipates the eschatological dawn when Christ, the Morning Star, illumines believers' hearts fully.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: knowing this first, that no prophecy of Scripture comes about by one's own interpretation.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For not by the will of man was prophecy ever brought forth, but being carried by the Holy Spirit, men spoke from God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Prophecy originates not from human will but from the Holy Spirit who "carried" (φερόμενοι, pheromenoi) the prophets—the same verb used of a ship driven by wind (Acts 27:15, 17). This affirms divine inspiration while preserving the human authors as conscious agents. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit of God who moved over the waters in creation (Gen 1:2) is the same Spirit who moved through the prophets, revealing the counsel of God to humanity. This establishes the authority of all Old Testament Scripture as originating "from God" (ἀπὸ θεοῦ), not from private human speculation.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But there arose also false prophets among the people, as also among you there shall be false teachers, who shall privily bring in destructive heresies, even denying the Master who bought them, bringing upon themselves swift destruction.
verses.note: GREEK: "False teachers" (ψευδοδιδάσκαλοι, pseudodidaskaloi) is a NT hapax legomenon, found only here. "Master" (δεσπότην, despotēn, Strong's G1203) denotes absolute ownership, stronger than kyrios—emphasizing Christ's sovereign claim over those He purchased (ἀγοράσαντα, agorasanta, "bought," marketplace language). THEOLOGICAL: The denial of Christ's lordship is the core heresy; these teachers reject the very One whose redemptive purchase gives Him rightful authority over them (cf. 1 Cor 6:20, Jude 4).

verses.flow: And many shall follow after their sensual indulgences, on account of whom the way of the truth shall be blasphemed.
And in covetousness with fabricated words they shall make merchandise of you; for whom the judgment from of old is not idle, and their destruction does not slumber.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For if God did not spare angels who sinned, but having cast them into Tartarus, delivered them to chains of deep darkness, being kept unto judgment;
verses.note: GREEK: "Cast into Tartarus" (ταρταρώσας, tartarōsas) is a NT hapax legomenon (Strong's G5020), the only use of this verb in all of Scripture. Tartarus in Greek cosmology was the lowest region of the underworld, beneath Hades. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This refers to the rebellious divine beings of Genesis 6:1-4 and 1 Enoch 6-16, heavenly watchers who transgressed their proper boundary. Peter draws on Second Temple tradition (1 Enoch, Jubilees) where these fallen angels are bound in chains of darkness awaiting final judgment. The "chains of deep darkness" (σειραῖς ζόφου) echo the binding imagery of 1 Enoch 10:4-6.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and did not spare the ancient world, but preserved Noah, an eighth person, a herald of righteousness, having brought a flood upon the world of the ungodly;
verses.note: GREEK: "Herald" (κήρυκα, kēryка, Strong's G2783) denotes a public proclaimer or official announcer, not merely a teacher. "Eighth" (ὄγδοον) means Noah plus seven others were preserved. TYPOLOGY: Noah as a preacher of righteousness foreshadows Christ's proclamation; the flood as judgment upon the ungodly world prefigures the coming eschatological judgment (cf. Matt 24:37-39, 1 Pet 3:20-21).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural neuter
verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and having reduced to ashes the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah, condemned them with an overthrow, having set them as an example unto those who should live ungodly;
verses.note: GREEK: "Reduced to ashes" (τεφρώσας, tephrōsas) is a NT hapax legomenon, derived from tephra ("ash"). "Example" (ὑπόδειγμα, hypodeigma) indicates Sodom and Gomorrah serve as a visible, historical pattern of divine judgment upon the ungodly. THEOLOGICAL: The destruction functions as a typological warning—what befell these cities foreshadows the eschatological fate of all who practice ungodliness (cf. Jude 7).

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: and delivered righteous Lot, who was oppressed by the conduct of the lawless in sensuality—

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: for by sight and by hearing, that righteous man, dwelling among them day after day, was tormenting his righteous soul by their lawless deeds—

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: the Lord knows how to deliver the godly out of trial, and to keep the unrighteous under punishment unto the day of judgment,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the theological conclusion of the extended conditional sentence begun in v. 4. The three historical examples (fallen angels, the flood, Sodom) demonstrate a twofold divine pattern: God simultaneously delivers the godly and reserves the unrighteous for judgment. GREEK: "Under punishment" (κολαζομένους, kolazomenous) is a present passive participle, indicating the unrighteous are presently being punished while being kept for the final day of judgment—an intermediate state of ongoing penalty.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: but especially those who walk after the flesh in the lust of defilement and despise authority. Daring, self-willed, they do not tremble when blaspheming glories,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Glories" (δόξας, doxas) likely refers to angelic beings or celestial powers, not abstract concepts of glory. These false teachers blaspheme heavenly beings they do not understand. GREEK: "Authority" (κυριότητος, kyriotētos, Strong's G2963) may refer to the lordship of Christ or to a class of angelic beings called "dominions" (cf. Eph 1:21, Col 1:16, Jude 8). The parallel with Jude 8-9 strongly supports an angelic referent.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: whereas angels, being greater in strength and power, do not bring against them a blasphemous judgment before the Lord.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But these, as irrational creatures, born naturally for capture and destruction, blaspheming in things which they are ignorant of, shall in their corruption also be destroyed,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: receiving the wages of unrighteousness, accounting revelry in the daytime a pleasure; spots and blemishes, reveling in their deceptions while feasting together with you,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: having eyes full of an adulteress and unceasing from sin, enticing unstable souls, having a heart trained in covetous practices—children of a curse!

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Having forsaken the straight way, they went astray, having followed the way of Balaam the son of Bosor, who loved the wages of unrighteousness,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Balaam son of Beor (Num 22-24) is a paradigmatic figure of the prophet-for-hire who leads God's people into sin for personal profit. HISTORICAL: "Bosor" is a variant form of "Beor"; some scholars suggest it reflects an Aramaic or Galilean pronunciation. The "way of Balaam" became a standard Jewish and early Christian cipher for those who corrupt the faithful for monetary gain (cf. Num 31:16, Rev 2:14, Jude 11).

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: but received a rebuke for his own transgression: a voiceless donkey, having spoken with a human voice, restrained the madness of the prophet.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: These are waterless springs and mists driven by a storm, for whom the deep darkness of darkness has been reserved.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For speaking great swelling words of emptiness, they entice by lusts of the flesh, by sensual indulgences, those who are just barely escaping from those who live in error,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: promising them freedom while they themselves are slaves of corruption; for by whom a man is overcome, by this one also he has been enslaved.
verses.note: GREEK: "Overcome" (ἥττηται, hēttētai, Strong's G2274) means to be defeated or made inferior. "Enslaved" (δεδούλωται, dedoulōtai) is a perfect passive, indicating a settled state of bondage. THEOLOGICAL: The grim irony is that these false teachers promise liberty (ἐλευθερίαν) while themselves being slaves of corruption (φθορᾶς). This echoes Jesus' teaching that everyone who commits sin is a slave of sin (John 8:34).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For if, having escaped the defilements of the world through the full knowledge of the Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, yet having become entangled again in these things, they are overcome, the last things have become for them worse than the first.
verses.note: GREEK: "Full knowledge" (ἐπιγνώσει, epignōsei, Strong's G1922) is a strengthened form of gnōsis, denoting thorough, experiential knowledge—not mere acquaintance. "Defilements" (μιάσματα, miasmata) is a NT hapax. THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents one of the most sobering warnings in the NT: those who have come to genuine knowledge of the Lord Jesus Christ and then return to entanglement in worldly corruption are in a worse state than before their knowledge. The passage echoes the Lord's teaching in Matt 12:45 and Luke 11:26.

words.m: verb aorist active participle dative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: For it would have been better for them not to have fully known the way of righteousness, than having fully known it, to turn back from the holy commandment delivered unto them.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: There has happened unto them that of the true proverb: "A dog having returned to its own vomit," and, "A sow, having washed, unto the wallowing-place of mire."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The first proverb echoes Proverbs 26:11 (LXX); the second, about the sow, is not found in the OT and likely derives from extrabiblical tradition, possibly a popular Semitic saying. GREEK: "Wallowing-place" (κυλισμόν, kulismon) is a NT hapax legomenon. THEOLOGICAL: Both images illustrate that the nature of the false teachers has not truly changed—the dog and the sow act according to their nature, returning to filth despite temporary cleansing. This raises profound questions about the genuineness of their earlier profession.

verses.flow: This already, beloved, a second letter I write to you, in which I stir up your pure mind by way of remembrance,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive plural neuter

verses.flow: to remember the words spoken beforehand by the holy prophets and the commandment of the Lord and Savior through your apostles,
verses.note: GREEK: "Proeirēmenōn" (προειρημένων, from προλέγω, Strong's G4302) means "spoken beforehand," emphasizing prophetic foreknowledge. The genitive chain "tēs tōn apostolōn hymōn entolēs tou kyriou kai sōtēros" is complex — the commandment belongs to the Lord and Savior, mediated through the apostles. THEOLOGICAL: Peter places apostolic teaching on par with Old Testament prophetic authority, grounding both in the Lord's own word.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: knowing this first, that in the last of the days scoffers shall come with scoffing, walking according to their own desires,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and saying, "Where is the promise of his coming? For from the day the fathers fell asleep, all things continue thus from the beginning of creation."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The scoffers' argument is uniformitarian — "all things continue thus from the beginning of creation" — denying divine intervention in history. This challenges both the Second Coming (parousia) and God's sovereign governance of the cosmos. TYPOLOGY: The appeal to "the fathers" falling asleep echoes patriarchal death language (Gen 15:15; Deut 31:16), but the scoffers weaponize it against hope.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: For this escapes their notice, willingly, that heavens existed of old and an earth out of water and through water, having been held together by the word of God,
verses.note: GREEK: "Lanthanei" (λανθάνει, Strong's G2990) means "it escapes notice" or "is hidden from them," and "thelontas" (θέλοντας) specifies this ignorance is willful — "desiring" it to be so. THEOLOGICAL: Peter refutes uniformitarianism by appealing to creation: the heavens and earth were constituted "out of water and through water by the word of God" (Gen 1:2, 6-9). The same divine word that created can also destroy and remake.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: through which the world at that time, having been flooded by water, perished.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The Flood (Gen 6-9) serves as the decisive counter-example to the scoffers' claim that God has never intervened catastrophically. "Kataklustheis" (κατακλυσθείς, Strong's G2626) is the verbal form behind "cataclysm." The Noahic judgment typologically foreshadows the eschatological judgment by fire (v.7), establishing a pattern: creation, judgment, re-creation.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But the present heavens and the earth by the same word have been stored up for fire, being kept unto the day of judgment and destruction of ungodly men.
But let not this one thing escape your notice, beloved, that one day with the Lord is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day.
verses.note: GREEK: Direct quotation of Psalm 90:4 (LXX 89:4). "Chillia etē" (χίλια ἔτη) = "a thousand years." THEOLOGICAL: Peter does not propose a mathematical formula for calculating the parousia but rather asserts God's radical transcendence over time. What appears as delay from a human perspective is not slowness from the divine perspective. This verse became foundational for later millennial discussions.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The Lord is not slow concerning the promise, as some count slowness, but is longsuffering toward you, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Makrothymei" (μακροθυμεῖ, Strong's G3114) — "is longsuffering" — reframes apparent delay as merciful patience. The divine will is expressed in two complementary purposes: "not willing that any should perish" and "that all should come to repentance" (metanoian, μετάνοιαν). GREEK: "Boulomenos" (βουλόμενος, Strong's G1014) denotes deliberate intention or counsel, not mere wish — God's patience is purposeful and salvific.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: But the day of the Lord shall come as a thief, in which the heavens shall pass away with a rushing noise, and the elements being burned up shall be dissolved, and the earth and the works in it shall be found out.
verses.note: GREEK: "Rhoizēdon" (ῥοιζηδόν, Strong's G4500) is a hapax legomenon in the NT — occurring only here — meaning "with a rushing noise" or "with a great roar," an onomatopoeic term evoking the violent sound of cosmic dissolution. "Stoicheia" (στοιχεῖα, Strong's G4747) may denote elemental substances or cosmic powers. TYPOLOGY: "The day of the Lord" echoes the prophetic yom YHWH tradition (Isa 13:6; Joel 2:1; Amos 5:18), now applied to Christ's return.

words.m: verb present passive participle genitive plural neuter

verses.flow: All these things thus being dissolved, what manner of persons ought you to be in holy conduct and godliness,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: expecting and hastening the coming of the day of God, on account of which the heavens being set on fire shall be dissolved, and the elements being burned up shall melt?
But new heavens and a new earth according to his promise we await, in which righteousness dwells.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "New heavens and a new earth" directly echoes Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22 (LXX), the prophetic promise of cosmic renewal. "Kainos" (καινός, Strong's G2537) denotes qualitative newness — not merely another cosmos but a transformed one. THEOLOGICAL: "In which righteousness dwells" (dikaiosynē katoikei) indicates not merely the absence of sin but the permanent habitation of divine justice — the telos of redemption.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, beloved, expecting these things, be diligent to be found by him spotless and blameless, in peace.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: And count the longsuffering of our Lord as salvation, just as also our beloved brother Paul, according to the wisdom given to him, wrote to you,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Peter's reference to Paul as "our beloved brother" and to "the wisdom given to him" is remarkable — it acknowledges Paul's apostolic authority while placing his letters alongside authoritative teaching. This is one of the earliest attestations of a Pauline letter collection. THEOLOGICAL: "Count the longsuffering of our Lord as salvation" links back to v.9, interpreting divine patience through a soteriological lens.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: as also in all his letters, speaking in them concerning these things, in which are some things hard to understand, which the unlearned and unstable distort, as also the rest of the Scriptures, to their own destruction.
verses.note: GREEK: "Dysnóēta" (δυσνόητα, Strong's G1425) — "hard to understand" — is a hapax legomenon in the NT. "Strebloōsin" (στρεβλοῦσιν, Strong's G4761) means "to twist" or "to distort," used of torture on the rack — a violent metaphor for mishandling Scripture. THEOLOGICAL: Peter explicitly categorizes Paul's letters alongside "the rest of the Scriptures" (tas loipas graphas), an extraordinary early witness to the emerging canonical status of apostolic writings.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: You therefore, beloved, knowing beforehand, guard yourselves, lest being carried away by the error of the lawless, you fall from your own steadfastness.
But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be the glory both now and unto the day of eternity. Amen.
verses.note: GREEK: "Auxanete" (αὐξάνετε, Strong's G837) is present imperative — "keep on growing." THEOLOGICAL: The doxology "to him be the glory both now and unto the day of eternity" ascribes to Christ the glory that belongs to God alone, a profound Christological affirmation. "Hēmeran aiōnos" (ἡμέραν αἰῶνος) — "the day of eternity" — is a striking phrase blending temporal and eternal categories, pointing to the consummation when time yields to everlasting reality.
